title: SEGMENT 2 — The PacBio idea: watch a polymerase work, in real time, on a single molecule

lines.text: SEGMENT 2 — The PacBio idea: watch a polymerase work, in real time, on a single molecule
So before we look at the physics, let me give you the central idea of PacBio in one sentence, because the elegance of the idea is genuinely worth pausing over.
The idea is this.
Take one single molecule of DNA polymerase.
Stick it to the bottom of a tiny well.
Give it a template strand to copy.
Provide it with fluorescently labeled nucleotides — each base type carries a different color of dye.
Then watch, under a microscope, as the polymerase incorporates one nucleotide at a time.
Each incorporation produces a flash of color.
Red flash means a T was added.
Green flash means a C.
And so on.
Record the sequence of flashes, and you've read the sequence of the DNA.
That's it.
That's the whole concept.
Sequencing by watching a single polymerase work in real time.
Stay with me.
Now, every single piece of that sentence sounds straightforward and is, in practice, fiendishly hard.
Let me walk through why.
First — you have to be able to see a single molecule of fluorescent dye.
Not a million copies.
One.
Not a population.
A single molecule, when it's flashing for a few milliseconds in solution.
To see a single molecule of fluorescence is already, by itself, the kind of thing that would have won a Nobel Prize twenty years before PacBio existed.
It requires extremely sensitive cameras, low backgrounds, and clever optics.
But that's just the entry fee.
Second — the polymerase is constantly being bombarded by free nucleotides floating around in solution.
To do its job, the polymerase needs a pool of all four nucleotides at fairly high concentration, micromolar at minimum, hovering around it ready to be grabbed.
Now, every one of those free nucleotides is carrying a fluorescent dye.

If you tried to image the polymerase under a normal microscope, the background fluorescence from millions of unincorporated nucleotides drifting through the field of view would completely swamp the signal from the one nucleotide actually being incorporated.
It would be like trying to spot a candle in a stadium full of stadium lights.
The signal-to-background problem is catastrophic.
Third — you need to know that the flash you just saw came from the correct molecule, not from some other reaction happening nearby.
If you have thousands of polymerases all working at once and you want to read each one's output independently, you can't have them confusing each other's signals.
Fourth — the polymerase itself has to be fast enough to be practical but slow enough that you can see each step.
If it incorporates a hundred bases a second, that's two milliseconds per base, which is doable with a fast camera, but tight.
If it incorporates ten bases a second, your sequencing throughput collapses.
There's a sweet spot.
Fifth — you need a polymerase that can keep going for a long, long time on a single template, without falling off, without getting tired, without losing accuracy.
Most polymerases run for a few hundred bases and stop.
You want one that runs for tens of thousands of bases, on the same molecule, without giving up.
Each of these problems, individually, was a research challenge.
Solving all of them together, in one integrated instrument — that's what PacBio spent more than a decade and several hundred million dollars doing.
The conceptual breakthrough that made the whole approach possible was the zero-mode waveguide, which we're going to spend the next segment on.

The zero-mode waveguide solves the background problem — it lets you illuminate only a tiny volume right at the bottom of a well, the volume where the polymerase sits, so unincorporated nucleotides floating around above the polymerase never get hit by the laser and never fluoresce.
The result is that essentially the only fluorescence you see is from the dye actually attached to the nucleotide currently being incorporated by the polymerase.
Background problem solved.
The polymerase problem was solved by adapting an enzyme called the phi29 polymerase — pronounced "phi twenty-nine" — which is a real workhorse of an enzyme borrowed from a bacterial virus.

Phi29 has the unusual properties of being extremely processive (meaning it stays on the template for a long time without falling off), and being able to displace any strand it bumps into as it moves along (which becomes crucial for circular templates).
We'll meet phi29 in segment six.
PacBio has further engineered it to slow it down a bit, make it more error-tolerant, and make it work with their specific chemistry.

The chemistry problem — how to attach a dye to a nucleotide in a way that the dye lights up briefly during incorporation and then disappears — was solved by attaching the dye to the phosphate group of the nucleotide.
We'll see that in segment five.
When the polymerase incorporates the nucleotide, the natural reaction cleaves off the phosphate group, taking the dye with it.
So the dye is present, attached, fluorescing only during the moment of incorporation, and then it's gone.
The result is a clean, time-resolved pulse of color, one per base.
Beautiful chemistry.

So the PacBio idea, packaged up, is: zero-mode waveguides plus phi29 polymerase plus phospholinked fluorescent nucleotides, all integrated on a chip with millions of independent wells, observed in parallel by a camera.
That's the platform.
The product is a real-time movie of polymerase activity, played back as a sequence of colored flashes, which the software translates into a base sequence.
Now, here's the thing I want you to feel about this approach, before we go into the details.
It's a fundamentally different paradigm from short-read sequencing.
Illumina, Sanger, and every other short-read method works by amplifying DNA — making many many many copies — and reading the average signal from that population.
The signal is loud because it's redundant.
PacBio doesn't amplify.
It reads a single molecule, the original one, directly.
There's no PCR step, no amplification bias, no copying errors introduced by polymerases other than the sequencing polymerase itself.
What you're reading is the actual DNA molecule that came out of the cell.
That's a profound difference.

We'll come back to its implications throughout the lecture — especially when we get to methylation calling, where the lack of amplification turns out to be a huge advantage because PCR erases methylation marks.
So that's the idea.
Now let's look at the physics that makes it possible.
